channel.title: breaking mitigations — algoatson.github.io
channel.description: ARMv8.3 signs pointers with a cryptographic MAC in their spare bits; ARMv8.5 tags every allocation with a 4-bit lock the pointer must match. Both are real upgrades over the stack canary — and both are beaten by the same old moves: a leak, a signing gadget, or a brute force the field is too small to resist.

item.title: the stack canary, from the source
item.description: Follow one random value from the kernel's AT_RANDOM, through glibc startup, into fs:0x28, and out to every function prologue. Reading the source explains the canary's quirks — the null low byte, the per-process (not per-call) value — and each quirk is a bypass.

item.title: PTR_MANGLE and hijacking the exit handlers
item.description: glibc stores some function pointers — atexit handlers, TLS destructors, jmp_buf — mangled with a secret guard so you can't just overwrite them with a target. Read how the mangle works, where its guard lives (fs:0x30, the canary's neighbour), and the two moves that defeat it: leak the guard, or zero it.

item.title: CET — shadow stacks, IBT, and what still works
item.description: Intel CET puts return addresses on a second, hardware-protected stack and demands every indirect branch land on an endbr64. That ends classic ROP and JOP. It does not end exploitation — whole-function calls, data-only attacks, and the occasional shadow-stack write all walk right past it.

item.title: PAC and MTE — how ARM64's new walls fall
item.description: ARMv8.3 signs pointers with a cryptographic MAC in their spare bits; ARMv8.5 tags every allocation with a 4-bit lock the pointer must match. Both are real upgrades over the stack canary — and both are beaten by the same old moves: a leak, a signing gadget, or a brute force the field is too small to resist.
